video_links.title: Alphablocks - Letter M
video_links.description: Alphablocks Red Level - Letter M introduction

worksheet_content.tracing:
Practice tracing: M M M m m m
Trace and say the sound: /m/ /m/ /m/
worksheet_content.practice: Circle the pictures that start with /m/: moon, mouse, map, milk
worksheet_content.writing: Write the letter: M m M m M m

teaching_notes.what_to_say: This letter makes the /m/ sound like in 'mom' and 'mouse'. Put your lips together and hum.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Show how lips come together. Have child feel vibration on lips. Practice sustained /mmm/ sound before short /m/.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Children may say the letter name instead of the sound. Remind them it's /m/ not 'em'.

worksheet_content.tracing:
Practice tracing: S S S s s s
Trace and say the sound: /s/ /s/ /s/
worksheet_content.practice: Circle the pictures that start with /s/: sun, snake, sock, sandwich
worksheet_content.writing: Write the letter: S s S s S s

teaching_notes.what_to_say: This letter makes the /s/ sound like a snake hissing. Put your tongue behind your top teeth and blow air.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Demonstrate tongue position. Have child make hissing sound. Practice continuous /sss/ before short /s/.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: May confuse with /z/ sound. Emphasize voiceless quality - no vibration in throat.

worksheet_content.tracing:
Practice tracing: A A A a a a
Trace and say the sound: /a/ /a/ /a/ (as in 'apple')
worksheet_content.practice: Circle the pictures with short /a/: cat, bat, hat, pan
worksheet_content.writing: Write the letter: A a A a A a

teaching_notes.what_to_say: This letter makes the /a/ sound like in 'apple' and 'cat'. Open your mouth wide.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Show wide open mouth. Not 'ay' (letter name) but /a/ (short sound). Exaggerate the short sound.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Confusion between short /a/ and long 'a'. Emphasize this is the short sound heard in 'cat', not 'cake'.

worksheet_content.tracing:
Practice tracing: T T T t t t
Trace and say the sound: /t/ /t/ /t/
worksheet_content.practice: Circle the pictures that start with /t/: tent, turtle, table, tiger
worksheet_content.writing: Write the letter: T t T t T t

teaching_notes.what_to_say: This letter makes the /t/ sound. Put your tongue behind your top teeth and pop it away.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Show tongue position. Practice explosive /t/ sound with puff of air. Feel the burst of air on hand.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: May say 'tuh' instead of clean /t/. Emphasize no added vowel sound after the /t/.

video_links.title: Blending CVC Words - Jack Hartmann
video_links.description: How to blend letter sounds together

worksheet_content.tracing: Trace the word: sat sat sat
worksheet_content.practice:
Sound it out: /s/ /a/ /t/ = sat
Circle the word 'sat' in sentences:
The cat sat. I sat down.
worksheet_content.writing: Write the word three times: sat ___ ___ ___

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Let's blend these sounds together smoothly. Start with /sss/ then add /aaa/ then /t/. Don't stop between sounds - blend them together: sssaaattt = sat!
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Point to each letter as you say its sound. Slide your finger smoothly under the word. Model continuous blending, not choppy sounds.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Choppy blending (s-a-t instead of sat). Encourage continuous sound blending without pauses between phonemes.

worksheet_content.tracing:
Practice tracing: P P P p p p
Trace and say the sound: /p/ /p/ /p/
worksheet_content.practice: Circle pictures starting with /p/: pig, pan, pizza, penguin
worksheet_content.writing: Write the letter: P p P p P p

teaching_notes.what_to_say: This letter makes the /p/ sound. Press your lips together and pop them open.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Show lip closure. Practice popping sound with air puff. Compare to /b/ but without voice.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: May confuse with /b/. Emphasize /p/ is voiceless - no throat vibration. Use hand to feel puff of air.

worksheet_content.practice:
Sound out each word:
/p/ /a/ /t/ = pat
/t/ /a/ /p/ = tap
/s/ /a/ /p/ = sap

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Notice how changing the first or last letter makes a completely different word! Let's sound out each one carefully.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Point out letter position changes. Practice blending each word smoothly. Show how same letters make different words.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Rushing through without careful blending. Remind child to touch each letter and say its sound before blending.

worksheet_content.tracing:
Practice tracing: I I I i i i
Trace and say: /i/ /i/ /i/ (as in 'igloo')
worksheet_content.practice: Circle pictures with short /i/: pig, fish, dig, bit
worksheet_content.writing: Write the letter: I i I i I i

teaching_notes.what_to_say: This letter makes the /i/ sound like in 'igloo' and 'sit'. Your mouth is slightly open and relaxed.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Demonstrate relaxed mouth position. This is NOT 'eye' (letter name) but /i/ (short sound). Practice with familiar words: sit, hit, kit.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Saying long 'i' sound (like 'ice'). Emphasize short, quick /i/ as in 'sit' not 'site'.

worksheet_content.tracing:
Practice tracing: N N N n n n
Trace and say: /n/ /n/ /n/
worksheet_content.practice: Circle pictures starting with /n/: nose, nest, net, nut
worksheet_content.writing: Write the letter: N n N n N n

teaching_notes.what_to_say: This letter makes the /n/ sound. Put your tongue on the roof of your mouth and hum through your nose.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Show tongue position touching alveolar ridge. Have child feel nose vibration while making /nnn/ sound.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: May confuse with /m/. Show difference - /n/ has tongue up, /m/ has lips together.

video_links.title: Short I Word Families - Jack Hartmann

worksheet_content.practice:
Blend these words:
/s/ /i/ /t/ = sit
/p/ /i/ /n/ = pin
/t/ /i/ /n/ = tin
worksheet_content.writing: Write each word twice: sit ___ pin ___ tin ___

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Let's read these short i words. Remember to blend smoothly from one sound to the next.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Emphasize short /i/ sound. Practice word families (-it, -in, -ip). Point to each letter while blending.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Substituting long 'i' sound. Reinforce short /i/ by comparing: 'sit' not 'site', 'pin' not 'pine'.

worksheet_content.tracing:
Practice tracing: C C C c c c
Trace and say: /k/ /k/ /k/
worksheet_content.practice: Circle pictures with hard C: cat, car, cup, corn
worksheet_content.writing: Write the letter: C c C c C c

teaching_notes.what_to_say: The letter C can make different sounds. Right now we're learning the /k/ sound like in 'cat' and 'cup'.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Demonstrate back-of-throat /k/ sound. Explain C also makes /s/ sound (in 'city') but we'll learn that later.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: May try to use soft C (/s/) sound. For now, stick with hard C before a, o, u.

worksheet_content.tracing:
Practice tracing: H H H h h h
Trace and say: /h/ /h/ /h/
worksheet_content.practice: Circle pictures starting with /h/: house, horse, hand, heart
worksheet_content.writing: Write the letter: H h H h H h

teaching_notes.what_to_say: This letter makes the /h/ sound like you're breathing out or panting. It's a whisper sound.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Show open mouth with breath. Practice panting like a dog: /h/ /h/ /h/. Very light sound, no harsh scraping.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Making sound too harsh. Should be gentle breath, not forced from throat.

worksheet_content.tracing:
Practice tracing: O O O o o o
Trace and say: /o/ /o/ /o/ (as in 'octopus')
worksheet_content.practice: Circle pictures with short /o/: dog, sock, pot, mop
worksheet_content.writing: Write the letter: O o O o O o

teaching_notes.what_to_say: This letter makes the /o/ sound like in 'octopus' and 'hot'. Open your mouth in a circle and say /o/.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Show rounded, open mouth. NOT 'oh' (letter name) but /o/ (short sound). Practice: hot, pot, dot.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Using long O sound ('oh'). Emphasize short /o/ as in 'hot' not 'hose'.

title: Blending: CAT, COT, HOT
phonics_concept: CVC words with short a and o

worksheet_content.practice:
Sound out:
/k/ /a/ /t/ = cat
/k/ /o/ /t/ = cot
/h/ /o/ /t/ = hot

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Watch how changing the vowel changes the word! Cat has /a/ but cot has /o/. Listen carefully to the middle sound.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Emphasize vowel differences. Have child feel mouth position change between /a/ and /o/. Practice minimal pairs.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Confusing short a and short o sounds. Use exaggerated articulation to show difference.

worksheet_content.tracing:
Practice tracing: R R R r r r
Trace and say: /r/ /r/ /r/
worksheet_content.practice: Circle pictures starting with /r/: rabbit, ring, rose, rocket
worksheet_content.writing: Write the letter: R r R r R r

teaching_notes.what_to_say: This letter makes the /r/ sound like a growling dog. Curl your tongue back a little.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Show tongue position - slightly curled or bunched. Practice /rrr/ sound. May be challenging - be patient.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: R is often difficult for young learners. Some may say /w/ instead. Model frequently and accept approximations initially.

worksheet_content.tracing:
Practice tracing: D D D d d d
Trace and say: /d/ /d/ /d/
worksheet_content.practice: Circle pictures starting with /d/: dog, door, duck, drum
worksheet_content.writing: Write the letter: D d D d D d

teaching_notes.what_to_say: This letter makes the /d/ sound. Put your tongue behind your top teeth like /t/ but add your voice.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Compare to /t/ - same position but voiced. Have child feel throat vibration for /d/ vs. none for /t/.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Confusing /d/ and /t/. Use hand on throat to feel vibration difference. Practice: 'duh' has buzz, 'tuh' doesn't.

worksheet_content.tracing:
Practice tracing: L L L l l l
Trace and say: /l/ /l/ /l/
worksheet_content.practice: Circle pictures starting with /l/: lion, lamp, leaf, ladder
worksheet_content.writing: Write the letter: L l L l L l

teaching_notes.what_to_say: This letter makes the /l/ sound. Put your tongue tip on the roof of your mouth and let the sound flow out the sides.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Show tongue touching alveolar ridge. Practice sustained /lll/ sound. Air flows around sides of tongue.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: May confuse with /r/ or /w/. Emphasize tongue-up position. Practice: 'luh' has tongue on roof, 'ruh' and 'wuh' don't.

worksheet_content.tracing:
Practice tracing: U U U u u u
Trace and say: /u/ /u/ /u/ (as in 'umbrella')
worksheet_content.practice: Circle pictures with short /u/: cup, sun, rug, bug
worksheet_content.writing: Write the letter: U u U u U u

teaching_notes.what_to_say: This letter makes the /u/ sound like in 'umbrella' and 'cup'. Your mouth is relaxed and slightly open.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Show relaxed, neutral mouth. NOT 'you' (letter name) but /u/ (short sound). Practice: cup, bug, run.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Saying long U ('you' sound). Emphasize short, quick /u/ as in 'cup' not 'cube'.

video_links.title: Short U Word Families - Jack Hartmann

worksheet_content.practice:
Blend these words:
/r/ /u/ /n/ = run
/s/ /u/ /n/ = sun
/f/ /u/ /n/ = fun
worksheet_content.writing: Write each word: run ___ sun ___ fun ___

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Let's practice our short u words! Remember, /u/ sounds like 'uh' in 'cup'.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Emphasize consistent short /u/ sound. Practice -un family words. Point and blend smoothly.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Using long U sound. Reinforce: 'run' not 'ruin', 'fun' not 'fewn'. Model correct short u repeatedly.

title: Review: All Short Vowels
phonics_concept: Comparing a, i, o, u sounds

book_recommendations.title: A wet dog and a fat hog

worksheet_content.practice:
Match vowels to words:
/a/ - cat, mat, hat
/i/ - sit, pin, lip
/o/ - hot, pot, dot
/u/ - run, sun, cup
worksheet_content.writing: Write one word for each vowel: a___ i___ o___ u___

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Let's review our vowel sounds! Each vowel makes its own special sound. Listen carefully to tell them apart.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Compare all four vowels learned so far. Use minimal pairs to highlight differences. Practice identifying vowel sounds in CVC words.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Mixing up vowel sounds. Use hand gestures or physical cues for each vowel to help differentiate.

book_recommendations.title: A fin is on the man

worksheet_content.tracing:
Practice tracing: B B B b b b
Trace and say: /b/ /b/ /b/
worksheet_content.practice: Circle pictures starting with /b/: ball, bear, book, banana
worksheet_content.writing: Write the letter: B b B b B b

teaching_notes.what_to_say: This letter makes the /b/ sound. Press your lips together and let them pop open with your voice.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Compare to /p/ - same lip position but /b/ uses voice. Have child feel throat vibration.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Confusing /b/ and /p/. Emphasize: /b/ has voice (buzzing in throat), /p/ doesn't. Practice alternating: /b/ /p/ /b/ /p/.

book_recommendations.title: A fin is on the man

worksheet_content.tracing:
Practice tracing: F F F f f f
Trace and say: /f/ /f/ /f/
worksheet_content.practice: Circle pictures starting with /f/: fish, fork, foot, fire
worksheet_content.writing: Write the letter: F f F f F f

teaching_notes.what_to_say: This letter makes the /f/ sound. Bite your bottom lip gently with your top teeth and blow air.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Show teeth-on-lip position. Practice continuous /fff/ sound like air escaping.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: May confuse with /v/. Emphasize /f/ is voiceless - no throat vibration, just air.

book_recommendations.title: A fin is on the man

worksheet_content.tracing:
Practice tracing: G G G g g g
Trace and say: /g/ /g/ /g/
worksheet_content.practice: Circle pictures with hard G: goat, girl, game, gas
worksheet_content.writing: Write the letter: G g G g G g

teaching_notes.what_to_say: The letter G makes the /g/ sound like in 'goat' and 'game'. It's a sound from the back of your throat.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Show back-of-throat position. Compare to /k/ but with voice. Have child feel throat vibration.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: May try soft G (/j/ sound). For now, stick with hard G before a, o, u. Explain soft G comes later.

book_recommendations.title: A fin is on the man

worksheet_content.tracing:
Practice tracing: W W W w w w
Trace and say: /w/ /w/ /w/
worksheet_content.practice: Circle pictures starting with /w/: wagon, watch, water, window
worksheet_content.writing: Write the letter: W w W w W w

teaching_notes.what_to_say: This letter makes the /w/ sound. Round your lips like you're going to whistle, then open them quickly.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Show rounded lips then quick release. Practice /w/ /w/ /w/ sound. Lips start together then move apart.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: May confuse with /v/. Show difference: /w/ starts with rounded lips, /v/ uses teeth on bottom lip.

book_recommendations.title: Ted was fed in his red bed

worksheet_content.tracing:
Practice tracing: E E E e e e
Trace and say: /e/ /e/ /e/ (as in 'egg')
worksheet_content.practice: Circle pictures with short /e/: bed, red, pen, nest
worksheet_content.writing: Write the letter: E e E e E e

teaching_notes.what_to_say: This letter makes the /e/ sound like in 'egg' and 'bed'. Your mouth is slightly open with your tongue relaxed.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Show mid-open mouth position. NOT 'ee' (letter name) but /e/ (short sound). Practice: bed, red, pet.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Using long E ('ee' sound). Emphasize short /e/ as in 'bed' not 'bead'. Model exaggerated short e.

video_links.title: Short E Word Families - Jack Hartmann

book_recommendations.title: Ted was fed in his red bed

worksheet_content.practice:
Blend these words:
/b/ /e/ /d/ = bed
/p/ /e/ /t/ = pet
/w/ /e/ /t/ = wet
worksheet_content.writing: Write each word: bed ___ pet ___ wet ___

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Now we have all 5 short vowels! Let's practice our short e words. Remember /e/ like in 'egg'.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Emphasize short /e/ sound. Practice -ed and -et families. Compare to other short vowels to reinforce differences.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Confusing short e with short i or short a. Use exaggerated articulation: 'bed' not 'bid' or 'bad'.

book_recommendations.title: A yak is in the jet

worksheet_content.tracing:
Practice tracing: J J J j j j
Trace and say: /j/ /j/ /j/
worksheet_content.practice: Circle pictures starting with /j/: jar, jump, jelly, jacket
worksheet_content.writing: Write the letter: J j J j J j

teaching_notes.what_to_say: This letter makes the /j/ sound like in 'jump' and 'jam'. It's similar to /ch/ but with your voice.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Compare to soft G sound (same sound, different spelling). Practice /j/ /j/ /j/. Voiced sound.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: May confuse with /ch/. Emphasize /j/ uses voice (buzzing), /ch/ doesn't. Practice: jump vs. chump.

book_recommendations.title: A yak is in the jet

worksheet_content.tracing:
Practice tracing: K K K k k k
Trace and say: /k/ /k/ /k/
worksheet_content.practice: Circle pictures starting with /k/: kite, key, king, kangaroo
worksheet_content.writing: Write the letter: K k K k K k

teaching_notes.what_to_say: The letter K makes the /k/ sound, just like hard C! Sometimes we use C, sometimes we use K for the same sound.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Explain K and C both make /k/. Generally K before i or e, C before a, o, u (simplified rule).
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Confusion about when to use K vs. C. For now, just recognize both make /k/ sound. Spelling rules come later.

book_recommendations.title: A yak is in the jet

worksheet_content.tracing:
Practice tracing: V V V v v v
Trace and say: /v/ /v/ /v/
worksheet_content.practice: Circle pictures starting with /v/: violin, vase, vacuum, vine
worksheet_content.writing: Write the letter: V v V v V v

teaching_notes.what_to_say: This letter makes the /v/ sound. It's like /f/ but with your voice buzzing.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Show teeth-on-lip position (same as F). But /v/ is voiced - add throat vibration. Compare: /f/ /v/ /f/ /v/.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Confusing /v/ and /f/. Use hand on throat to feel vibration for /v/, none for /f/. Practice: van vs. fan.

book_recommendations.title: A yak is in the jet

worksheet_content.tracing:
Practice tracing: Y Y Y y y y
Trace and say: /y/ /y/ /y/
worksheet_content.practice: Circle pictures starting with /y/: yak, yarn, yo-yo, yawn
worksheet_content.writing: Write the letter: Y y Y y Y y

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Y can be tricky! At the start of words, it makes the /y/ sound like in 'yes'. (Later we'll learn Y as a vowel.)
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Focus on consonant Y at word beginnings for now. Quick /y/ sound with tongue near roof of mouth.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Y is complex - can be consonant or vowel. For now, teach only initial Y as in 'yes, yellow, yell'.

book_recommendations.title: A yak is in the jet

worksheet_content.tracing:
Practice tracing: Z Z Z z z z
Trace and say: /z/ /z/ /z/
worksheet_content.practice: Circle pictures starting with /z/: zebra, zipper, zero, zigzag
worksheet_content.writing: Write the letter: Z z Z z Z z

teaching_notes.what_to_say: This letter makes the /z/ sound like a buzzing bee. It's like /s/ but with your voice.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Compare to /s/ - same tongue position but /z/ is voiced. Have child feel throat vibration. Practice: /sss/ /zzz/ /sss/ /zzz/.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Confusing /z/ and /s/. Use hand on throat: /z/ buzzes, /s/ doesn't. Practice: zip vs. sip.

book_recommendations.title: A yak is in the jet

worksheet_content.tracing:
Practice tracing: X X X x x x
Trace and say: /ks/ /ks/ /ks/
worksheet_content.practice: Circle pictures with X sound: box, fox, six, mix
worksheet_content.writing: Write the letter: X x X x X x

teaching_notes.what_to_say: X is special! It usually makes two sounds together: /k/ and /s/ = /ks/. Listen: bo/ks/, fo/ks/, si/ks/.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Explain X represents blend of /k/ + /s/. Usually at end of words. Practice saying /ks/ quickly together.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: X rarely starts words in English (except xylophone). Focus on X at word endings. X makes /ks/ not 'eks'.

video_links.title: Alphablocks - QU
video_links.description: Q and U are always together

worksheet_content.tracing:
Practice tracing: qu qu qu
Trace and say: /kw/ /kw/ /kw/
worksheet_content.practice: Circle QU words: queen, quilt, question, quick

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Q is a special letter - it's ALWAYS followed by U! Together they make the /kw/ sound like in 'queen' and 'quick'.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Teach Q and U as inseparable team. Sound is /k/ + /w/ = /kw/. Never use Q alone in English words.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Trying to use Q without U. Establish rule firmly: Q needs U, always. They're best friends!

video_links.title: Alphabet Song with Sounds - Jack Hartmann

worksheet_content.practice: Match each letter to its sound. Write one word for each letter of the alphabet.
worksheet_content.writing: Practice writing CVC words using all letters learned.

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Congratulations! You've learned all 26 letter sounds! Now you can read lots of words by blending sounds together.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Quick review of all consonants and 5 short vowels. Celebrate this milestone! Practice alphabet fluency.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Some letters may still be confused (b/d, p/q, m/n). Continue reinforcing through practice and multisensory activities.

worksheet_content.practice:
Sound it out: /s/ /t/ together = /st/
Blend: st-o-p = stop, st-e-p = step
worksheet_content.writing: Write ST words: stop ___ stem ___ step ___

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Now we're blending TWO consonants at the start! Say /s/ then quickly add /t/ - /ssstt/. Then add the rest of the word.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Demonstrate smooth blending of both consonants before vowel. This is CCVC pattern. Point to each letter while blending.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Adding vowel between consonants ('suh-tuh-op'). Emphasize: blend /s/ and /t/ smoothly together BEFORE the vowel.

worksheet_content.practice:
Blend: /s/ /p/ = /sp/
sp-i-n = spin, sp-o-t = spot

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Another S blend! /s/ and /p/ together make /sp/ like in 'spin' and 'spot'. Blend them smoothly!
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Practice /sssppp/ smoothly before adding vowel. Compare ST and SP blends to show pattern.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Separating the consonants. Encourage continuous blending: 'ssspp-i-n' not 's-p-i-n'.

worksheet_content.practice:
Blend: /s/ /n/ = /sn/
sn-a-p = snap, sn-u-g = snug

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Here's another S blend! /s/ and /n/ = /sn/ like in 'snap' and 'snack'. Say them together smoothly.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Show smooth transition from /sss/ to /nnn/. Practice SN family: snap, snack, snip, snug.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Difficulty blending /s/ and /n/. Use continuous sounds: /sssnnn-a-p/. Model slowly, then speed up.

worksheet_content.practice:
Blend: /p/ /l/ = /pl/
pl-a-n = plan, pl-u-m = plum

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Now we have L blends! /p/ and /l/ make /pl/ like in 'plan' and 'play'. Blend the two consonants together first.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Demonstrate /p/ flowing into /l/. L blends can be trickier - practice the blend separately before adding vowel.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Adding schwa between consonants ('puh-lan'). Emphasize: /pl/ is one smooth sound at the beginning.

book_recommendations.title: A crab is in the pram
book_recommendations.level: CCVC - tr, bl, pr, cr, cl

worksheet_content.practice:
Blend: /b/ /l/ = /bl/
bl-o-b = blob, bl-e-d = bled

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Another L blend! /b/ and /l/ together make /bl/ like in 'block' and 'blue'. Blend smoothly!
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Practice /b/ flowing into /l/. Compare to PL blend. Both use /l/ but different first consonant.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Inserting vowel: 'buh-lob'. Model correct blending: /bl/ as one unit at start of word.

book_recommendations.title: A crab is in the pram
book_recommendations.level: CCVC - tr, bl, pr, cr, cl

worksheet_content.practice:
Blend: /k/ /l/ = /kl/
cl-a-p = clap, cl-i-p = clip

teaching_notes.what_to_say: One more L blend! C and L make /kl/ like in 'clap' and 'clock'. Remember, C makes the /k/ sound here.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Emphasize hard C sound /k/ blended with /l/. Practice /kl/ family words.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Some children struggle with /kl/ blend. Practice in isolation, then in words. Model clearly.

book_recommendations.title: This frog gran will drum
book_recommendations.level: CCVC - fl, gr, dr, fr, sk, sw, br

worksheet_content.practice:
Blend: /f/ /l/ = /fl/
fl-a-g = flag, fl-i-p = flip

teaching_notes.what_to_say: F and L together make /fl/ like in 'flag' and 'flower'. Blend /f/ and /l/ smoothly together.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Show /fff/ flowing into /lll/. FL blend is common in English. Practice FL family.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Separating /f/ and /l/. Reinforce continuous blending from /f/ to /l/ to vowel.

worksheet_content.practice:
Blend: /g/ /l/ = /gl/
gl-a-d = glad, gl-e-n = glen

teaching_notes.what_to_say: G and L make /gl/ like in 'glad' and 'glow'. Remember, G makes the /g/ sound here, then blend with /l/.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Hard G /g/ plus /l/. Practice /gl/ blend. Less common but important to know.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Using soft G sound. Reinforce hard G /g/ in GL blends. Model: /gl/ not /jl/.

worksheet_content.practice:
Blend: /s/ /l/ = /sl/
sl-i-p = slip, sl-e-d = sled

teaching_notes.what_to_say: S and L together make /sl/ like in 'slip' and 'sleep'. Blend /sss/ and /lll/ smoothly!
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Continuous /sss/ flowing into /lll/. Both are continuants so easy to blend smoothly.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Choppy blending. Emphasize smooth transition: /sssllll-i-p/.

book_recommendations.title: A crab is in the pram
book_recommendations.level: CCVC - tr, bl, pr, cr, cl

worksheet_content.practice:
Blend: /k/ /r/ = /kr/
cr-a-b = crab, cr-o-p = crop

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Now we're learning R blends! C and R make /kr/ like in 'crab' and 'crown'. C makes /k/ sound, then add /r/.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Hard C /k/ plus /r/. R blends can be challenging. Practice /kr/ before adding to words.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: R blends are difficult for many children. Accept approximations initially. Model frequently: /kr/ not 'kur'.

book_recommendations.title: This frog gran will drum
book_recommendations.level: CCVC - fl, gr, dr, fr, sk, sw, br

worksheet_content.practice:
Blend: /b/ /r/ = /br/
br-a-g = brag, br-e-d = bred

teaching_notes.what_to_say: B and R together make /br/ like in 'brush' and 'bread'. Blend /b/ and /r/ together smoothly!
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: /b/ flowing into /r/. Practice BR family. Common R blend in English.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Adding vowel between: 'buh-rag'. Reinforce: /br/ is one smooth blend at the start.

book_recommendations.title: A crab is in the pram
book_recommendations.level: CCVC - tr, bl, pr, cr, cl

worksheet_content.practice:
Blend: /t/ /r/ = /tr/
tr-a-p = trap, tr-i-p = trip

teaching_notes.what_to_say: T and R make /tr/ like in 'tree' and 'truck'. /t/ then quickly /r/ - blend them together!
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: /t/ flowing into /r/. Very common blend. Practice TR family words.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Some children say 'chr' instead of /tr/. Model carefully: /tr/ not 'chur'.

book_recommendations.title: This frog gran will drum
book_recommendations.level: CCVC - fl, gr, dr, fr, sk, sw, br

worksheet_content.practice:
Blend: /d/ /r/ = /dr/
dr-i-p = drip, dr-u-m = drum

teaching_notes.what_to_say: D and R together make /dr/ like in 'drum' and 'drop'. Blend /d/ and /r/ smoothly!
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: /d/ flowing into /r/. Compare to TR blend - similar but DR is voiced.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: May say /jr/ instead of /dr/. Model clearly: /dr/ not 'jr'.

book_recommendations.title: This frog gran will drum
book_recommendations.level: CCVC - fl, gr, dr, fr, sk, sw, br

worksheet_content.practice:
Blend: /f/ /r/ = /fr/
fr-o-g = frog, fr-o-m = from

teaching_notes.what_to_say: F and R make /fr/ like in 'frog' and 'fruit'. Blend /fff/ and /rrr/ together smoothly!
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: /f/ flowing into /r/. Both continuous sounds make this easier to blend.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Adding schwa: 'fuh-rog'. Model continuous blending: /ffrrr-o-g/.

book_recommendations.title: This frog gran will drum
book_recommendations.level: CCVC - fl, gr, dr, fr, sk, sw, br

worksheet_content.practice:
Blend: /g/ /r/ = /gr/
gr-i-n = grin, gr-a-b = grab

teaching_notes.what_to_say: G and R together make /gr/ like in 'grass' and 'green'. Hard G /g/ plus /r/!
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Hard G /g/ blended with /r/. Practice /gr/ family words.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Using soft G. Reinforce hard G /g/ in GR blends.

book_recommendations.title: A crab is in the pram
book_recommendations.level: CCVC - tr, bl, pr, cr, cl

worksheet_content.practice:
Blend: /p/ /r/ = /pr/
pr-e-p = prep, pr-o-d = prod

teaching_notes.what_to_say: P and R make /pr/ like in 'present' and 'prize'. Blend /p/ and /r/ together!
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: /p/ flowing into /r/. Less common than other R blends but still important.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: R blends can all be challenging. Practice PR in isolation before words.

title: Review: All R Blends
phonics_concept: CR, BR, TR, DR, FR, GR, PR review

book_recommendations.title: A crab is in the pram

worksheet_content.tracing: Write R blends: cr br tr dr fr gr pr
worksheet_content.practice:
Match blend to word:
cr-crab, br-brag, tr-trap, dr-drip, fr-frog, gr-grin
worksheet_content.writing: Write one word for each R blend

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Great job learning all the R blends! These are some of the trickiest blends. Let's practice reading them in words.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Review each R blend family. Compare and contrast. Celebrate progress on difficult skill!
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: R blends remain challenging. Continued practice and patience needed. Multisensory activities help.

book_recommendations.title: This frog gran will drum
book_recommendations.level: CCVC - fl, gr, dr, fr, sk, sw, br

worksheet_content.practice:
Blend: /s/ /k/ = /sk/
sk-i-p = skip, sk-i-n = skin

teaching_notes.what_to_say: S and K together make /sk/ like in 'skip' and 'sky'. Blend /s/ and /k/ smoothly!
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: /s/ flowing into /k/. SK blend is common. Practice SK family words.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: May add vowel between: 'suh-kip'. Model smooth /sk/ blend.

book_recommendations.title: This frog gran will drum
book_recommendations.level: CCVC - fl, gr, dr, fr, sk, sw, br

worksheet_content.practice:
Blend: /s/ /w/ = /sw/
sw-i-m = swim, sw-e-pt = swept

teaching_notes.what_to_say: S and W make /sw/ like in 'swim' and 'swing'. Start with /s/ then add /w/ with rounded lips!
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: /s/ flowing into /w/. Show lip rounding for W while continuing /s/ sound.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Difficulty transitioning from /s/ to /w/. Practice in isolation, then in words.

worksheet_content.practice:
Blend: /s/ /m/ = /sm/
sm-o-g = smog, sm-u-g = smug

teaching_notes.what_to_say: S and M together make /sm/ like in 'smell' and 'smile'. Blend /sss/ into /mmm/ smoothly!
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Both are continuous sounds. /sss/ flows easily into /mmm/. Practice SM family.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Usually easier blend due to continuous sounds. Still model smooth blending.

worksheet_content.practice:
Blend: SC makes /sk/ sound
sc-a-n = scan, sc-a-t = scat

teaching_notes.what_to_say: SC is a special spelling! Sometimes SC makes the /sk/ sound like in 'scan'. (Sometimes it makes /s/ sound but we'll learn that later.)
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Explain SC = /sk/ before a, o, u. Same sound as SK, different spelling.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Confusion between SK and SC spellings. For now, just recognize both can make /sk/ sound.

title: Review: All S Blends
phonics_concept: ST, SP, SN, SL, SM, SK, SW, SC review

worksheet_content.tracing: Write S blends: st sp sn sl sm sk sw sc
worksheet_content.practice:
Match blend to word:
st-stop, sp-spin, sn-snap, sl-slip, sm-smell, sk-skip, sw-swim
worksheet_content.writing: Write one word for each S blend

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Excellent work on S blends! S can partner with many different consonants. Let's review them all.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Compare all S blends. Practice reading words with various S blends. Reinforce smooth blending.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: S blends are generally easier than R blends. Continue emphasizing no added vowel between consonants.

title: Review: All L Blends
phonics_concept: PL, BL, CL, FL, GL, SL review

book_recommendations.title: A crab is in the pram

worksheet_content.tracing: Write L blends: pl bl cl fl gl sl
worksheet_content.practice:
Match blend to word:
pl-plan, bl-blob, cl-clap, fl-flag, gl-glad, sl-slip
worksheet_content.writing: Write one word for each L blend

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Great job with L blends! L can partner with several consonants. Let's review all the L blend families.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Review each L blend. Compare and practice. L blends can be tricky - celebrate progress!
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: L blends challenging for many. Continue multisensory practice. Be patient with approximations.

worksheet_content.practice: Blend: a-nd = and, e-nd = end, se-nd = send

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Now we're learning blends at the END of words! -ND is /n/ and /d/ together at the end, like in 'and' and 'send'.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Show CVCC pattern. Blend beginning, then blend final consonants together. Point to each part while blending.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: May separate final blend. Emphasize smooth blending: 'se-nnd' not 'se-n-d'.

worksheet_content.practice: Blend: a-nt = ant, se-nt = sent, be-nt = bent

teaching_notes.what_to_say: -NT is /n/ and /t/ at the end, like in 'ant' and 'sent'. Blend them together smoothly!
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: CVCC pattern with -nt ending. Very common ending blend. Practice -nt family words.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Separating final consonants. Model continuous blending to the end.

worksheet_content.practice: Blend: ca-mp = camp, bu-mp = bump, ju-mp = jump

teaching_notes.what_to_say: -MP is /m/ and /p/ at the end, like in 'jump' and 'camp'. Put them together at the end!
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: CVCC with -mp ending. Common ending. Practice -mp family.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: May omit final /p/. Emphasize both sounds: /m/ flowing into /p/.

worksheet_content.practice: Blend: be-st = best, du-st = dust, fa-st = fast

teaching_notes.what_to_say: -ST at the end is /s/ and /t/ together, like in 'fast' and 'best'. Very common ending!
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: /s/ flowing into /t/ at word end. Very common. Practice -st family.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: May add extra syllable. 'Fast' is one syllable, not 'fas-tuh'.

worksheet_content.practice: Blend: be-lt = belt, me-lt = melt, ti-lt = tilt

teaching_notes.what_to_say: -LT is /l/ and /t/ at the end, like in 'belt' and 'melt'. Blend them together!
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: /l/ flowing into /t/. Practice -lt family words.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: May drop the /t/. Emphasize both final sounds clearly.

worksheet_content.practice: Blend: i-nk = ink, ba-nk = bank, si-nk = sink

teaching_notes.what_to_say: -NK is /n/ and /k/ together at the end. It's called a 'glued sound' because /n/ and /k/ stick together! Like in 'think' and 'bank'.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Teach as 'glued sound' or 'welded sound'. /ng/ sound (velar nasal) before /k/. Very common pattern.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: The /n/ in -nk sounds different than regular /n/ - it's actually /ng/. Model carefully: 'think' not 'thin-k'.

worksheet_content.practice:
Sound: NG makes one sound /ng/ (like the end of 'sing')
ri-ng = ring, so-ng = song, lu-ng = lung

teaching_notes.what_to_say: NG is special! Two letters but ONE sound: /ng/ like in 'sing' and 'ring'. It's actually a digraph, not a blend.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Emphasize NG = one sound (digraph), not two sounds blended. Nasal /ng/ sound from back of throat.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: May try to say /n/ + /g/ separately. Teach as single /ng/ sound. Model: 'sing' has /ng/ at end, not 'sin-g'.

worksheet_content.practice:
Sound: SH makes one sound /sh/ (like 'shh, be quiet!')
sh-o-p = shop, sh-i-p = ship, fi-sh = fish

teaching_notes.what_to_say: SH is our first digraph! Two letters that make ONE new sound: /sh/ like when you say 'shhh'. Not /s/ and /h/ - just /sh/!
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Emphasize digraph = two letters, one sound. /sh/ at beginning (shop) or end (fish). Model /sh/ sound.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Trying to blend /s/ and /h/. Teach SH as single unit making /sh/ sound. Use 'shh, be quiet' as memory aid.

worksheet_content.practice:
Sound: CH makes /ch/ (like a train: choo-choo!)
ch-a-t = chat, ch-i-p = chip, mu-ch = much

teaching_notes.what_to_say: CH is another digraph! Two letters making one sound: /ch/ like a train going 'choo-choo!' or when you sneeze 'achoo!'
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Digraph /ch/ at beginning (chip) or end (much). Not /k/ or /s/ - /ch/ sound. Use train sound as memory aid.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Confusing with /sh/ or hard C. Model clear /ch/ sound. Compare: 'chip' vs 'ship', 'chat' vs 'cat'.

worksheet_content.practice:
Sound: TH makes /th/ (stick tongue between teeth, add voice)
th-i-s = this, th-a-t = that, th-e-m = them

teaching_notes.what_to_say: TH is a special digraph with TWO sounds! First we'll learn the one with your voice buzzing: /th/ in 'this' and 'that'. Stick your tongue between your teeth and hum.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Show tongue between teeth. This TH is VOICED - throat vibrates. Teach common words: this, that, the, them.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: May substitute /d/ or /v/ for /th/. Model tongue placement carefully. Have child feel tongue between teeth.

worksheet_content.practice:
Sound: TH also makes /th/ (tongue between teeth, NO voice - just air)
th-i-n = thin, th-i-nk = think, pa-th = path

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Now the other TH sound! Same tongue position but NO voice this time - just air: /th/ in 'think' and 'bath'. It's like a whisper.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Show tongue between teeth. This TH is UNVOICED - no throat vibration, just air. Compare: 'this' (voiced) vs 'think' (unvoiced).
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: May substitute /f/ or /t/ for unvoiced TH. Emphasize tongue must be BETWEEN teeth, not behind them.

worksheet_content.practice:
Sound: WH makes /wh/ or /w/ (often sounds like /w/ in modern English)
wh-e-n = when, wh-a-t = what, wh-i-ch = which

teaching_notes.what_to_say: WH is a digraph found in question words! It usually sounds like /w/ in modern English: when, what, why, where. (Some people say /hw/ with a little /h/ first.)
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: In most dialects, WH = /w/ sound. Teach common WH question words. Historical /hw/ distinction rarely maintained.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Trying to say /w/ and /h/ separately. Teach WH words as sight words or /w/ sound for simplicity.

worksheet_content.practice:
Sound: CK makes /k/ at the END of words after short vowels
ba-ck = back, du-ck = duck, so-ck = sock

teaching_notes.what_to_say: CK is a special digraph! It only comes at the END of short vowel words and makes the /k/ sound. Like in 'duck', 'sock', 'back'. We use CK instead of just K after short vowels.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Spelling rule: use CK after short vowel, K after other patterns. CK = /k/ at word end. Very common pattern.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: May confuse CK, K, and C spellings of /k/. For now, learn CK pattern after short vowels.

title: Review: All Digraphs
phonics_concept: SH, CH, TH, WH, CK, NG review

worksheet_content.tracing: Write digraphs: sh ch th wh ck ng
worksheet_content.practice:
Match digraph to word:
sh-shop, ch-chip, th-this, th-thin, wh-when, ck-duck, ng-ring
worksheet_content.writing: Write one word for each digraph

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Excellent work learning digraphs! Remember: digraphs are two letters that make ONE sound. Let's review them all.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Compare all digraphs. Practice reading words with each. Emphasize two letters = one sound concept.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: May still try to blend digraph letters separately. Reinforce: digraphs are single sounds, not blends.

title: Silent E: A_E
phonics_concept: Long A with silent e (CVCe pattern)

video_links.title: Silent E Song - Jack Hartmann
video_links.description: Magic E makes vowels say their name

worksheet_content.tracing: Trace: a_e words: make cake lake
worksheet_content.practice:
Silent E rule: E at the end makes the vowel say its NAME!
mat → mate, cap → cape, mad → made

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Magic E! When E comes at the end, it makes the vowel say its NAME instead of its sound. The E is silent. So 'cap' becomes 'cape', 'mat' becomes 'mate'!
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Teach CVCe pattern. Compare CVC (short vowel) to CVCe (long vowel). Use 'magic' or 'bossy' E terminology. Show transformations.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: May read long vowel as short. Emphasize: silent E makes the vowel 'say its name' - long sound. Practice minimal pairs.

title: Silent E: I_E
phonics_concept: Long I with silent e (CVCe pattern)

worksheet_content.tracing: Trace: i_e words: bike like time
worksheet_content.practice:
Silent E makes I say its name: 'eye'
bit → bite, kit → kite, dim → dime

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Magic E works with I too! When E is at the end, I says its name 'eye' instead of /i/. So 'bit' becomes 'bite', 'kit' becomes 'kite'!
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: CVCe with long I. Compare minimal pairs: bit/bite, kit/kite, dim/dime. Silent E pattern consistent across vowels.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Confusing long I ('eye') with short /i/. Emphasize the transformation: silent E makes it say 'eye'.

title: Silent E: O_E
phonics_concept: Long O with silent e (CVCe pattern)

video_links.title: Magic E - Long O - Jack Hartmann

worksheet_content.tracing: Trace: o_e words: bone home hope
worksheet_content.practice:
Silent E makes O say its name: 'oh'
hop → hope, not → note, rob → robe

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Magic E with O! When E is at the end, O says its name 'oh' instead of /o/. So 'hop' becomes 'hope', 'not' becomes 'note'!
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: CVCe with long O. Compare CVC to CVCe. Practice minimal pairs: hop/hope, not/note, rob/robe.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Using short O sound. Remind: silent E makes O say 'oh', its letter name.

title: Silent E: U_E
phonics_concept: Long U with silent e (CVCe pattern)

video_links.title: Silent E - Long U - Jack Hartmann

worksheet_content.tracing: Trace: u_e words: cube cute tube
worksheet_content.practice:
Silent E makes U say its name: 'you'
cub → cube, cut → cute, tub → tube

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Magic E with U! When E is at the end, U says its name 'you' instead of /u/. So 'cub' becomes 'cube', 'cut' becomes 'cute'!
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: CVCe with long U ('you' sound or sometimes 'oo'). Compare minimal pairs: cub/cube, cut/cute, tub/tube.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Long U can sound like 'you' or 'oo' depending on word. Both are acceptable - context determines.

title: Silent E: E_E
phonics_concept: Long E with silent e (rare CVCe pattern)

worksheet_content.tracing: Trace: e_e words: these theme Pete
worksheet_content.practice:
Silent E makes E say its name: 'ee'
these, theme (less common pattern)

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Sometimes we see E_E pattern too! The second E is silent and makes the first E say 'ee'. This is less common than the other silent E patterns.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: E_E is rarer than other CVCe patterns. Teach a few common words: these, theme. Often found in names: Pete, Steve.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: E_E pattern is infrequent. Focus on other CVCe patterns primarily. Teach these words as examples.

worksheet_content.tracing: Write CVCe patterns: a_e i_e o_e u_e e_e
worksheet_content.practice:
Transform words with silent E:
mat→mate, bit→bite, hop→hope, cub→cube
worksheet_content.writing: Write one CVCe word for each vowel

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Fantastic! You've learned all the silent E patterns! Remember: the E at the end is silent and makes the vowel say its name. This is a BIG reading skill!
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Review all CVCe patterns. Practice identifying long vs short vowels. Use minimal pairs. Celebrate this major milestone!
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: May still confuse short and long vowels. Continue practice with minimal pairs. Reinforce CVCe pattern recognition.

video_links.title: When Two Vowels Go Walking - Between the Lions

worksheet_content.practice:
AI makes long A sound: 'ay'
rain, wait, pain, train, tail

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Now we're learning vowel teams! When A and I are together, they make the long A sound 'ay' like in 'rain' and 'train'. Remember: 'When two vowels go walking, the first one does the talking!'
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Vowel digraph AI = long A. Usually in middle of words. Teach 'two vowels walking' rule as helpful memory aid (though not 100% reliable).
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: May try to say both vowel sounds. Emphasize: AI together makes one sound - long A.

worksheet_content.practice:
AY makes long A sound: 'ay'
day, say, play, may, way

teaching_notes.what_to_say: AY is another way to spell long A! AY usually comes at the END of words, while AI is usually in the middle. Both say 'ay': rain, day.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Spelling pattern: AI in middle (rain, wait), AY at end (day, play). Both = long A sound.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Confusing AI and AY placement. Teach general rule: AI middle, AY end. Practice reading both patterns.

worksheet_content.practice:
EE makes long E sound: 'ee'
bee, see, feet, tree, green

teaching_notes.what_to_say: When two E's are together, they make the long E sound 'ee' like in 'bee' and 'tree'. Two E's work together to say 'eee'!
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Vowel team EE = long E. Very consistent pattern. Can appear in middle or end of words.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Usually straightforward. EE is one of most reliable vowel teams. Emphasize two letters, one sound.

worksheet_content.practice:
EA usually makes long E sound: 'ee'
eat, sea, read, teach, seat

teaching_notes.what_to_say: EA is another way to spell long E! Usually EA says 'ee' like in 'eat' and 'teach'. (Sometimes EA makes other sounds, but we'll learn those later.)
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: EA most commonly = long E. Note: EA can also make short E (bread) or long A (great), but teach long E first as most common.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: EA is less consistent than EE. Start with long E words. Introduce exceptions later (bread, head, great, steak).

worksheet_content.practice:
OA makes long O sound: 'oh'
boat, coat, road, soap, goat

teaching_notes.what_to_say: When O and A are together, they usually make the long O sound 'oh' like in 'boat' and 'road'. OA works as a team!
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Vowel team OA = long O. Very consistent pattern. Usually in middle of words.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: OA is quite reliable. Emphasize two vowels making one sound - long O.

video_links.title: OW Vowel Team - Jack Hartmann
video_links.description: OW can make long O or /ow/

worksheet_content.practice:
OW can make long O: 'oh'
low, snow, show, grow, yellow

teaching_notes.what_to_say: OW is tricky! Sometimes it makes long O like in 'snow' and 'yellow'. (Other times it makes the /ow/ sound like in 'cow' - we'll learn that later.)
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: OW has two sounds: long O (snow, show) and /ow/ diphthong (cow, now). Teach long O first. Generally: long O at end, /ow/ in middle (not absolute).
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: OW ambiguity is challenging. Students must try both sounds to determine correct pronunciation. Context helps.

worksheet_content.practice:
OO makes /oo/ sound (like 'moon')
moon, soon, food, cool, zoo

teaching_notes.what_to_say: OO can make two sounds! First let's learn the long /oo/ sound like in 'moon' and 'food'. Your lips make a circle shape: /oo/.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: OO has two pronunciations: long /oo/ (moon, food) and short /oo/ (book, good). Teach long /oo/ first as it's more common.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: OO ambiguity challenging. Students must try both sounds. No reliable rule for which OO sound to use.

worksheet_content.practice:
OO also makes /oo/ sound (like 'book' - shorter)
book, look, good, wood, foot

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Now the other OO sound! This one is shorter, like in 'book' and 'look'. Still round your lips but the sound is different from 'moon'.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Short /oo/ (book, took, good). Compare to long /oo/ (moon, food). Both use OO spelling. Context and familiarity help determine which.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Distinguishing OO sounds requires practice. Teach common words in each pattern. Students learn through exposure.

title: Review: All Long Vowel Teams
phonics_concept: AI, AY, EE, EA, OA, OW, OO review

worksheet_content.tracing: Write vowel teams: ai ay ee ea oa ow oo
worksheet_content.practice:
Match team to word:
ai-rain, ay-day, ee-tree, ea-sea, oa-boat, ow-snow, oo-moon
worksheet_content.writing: Write one word for each vowel team

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Wonderful work on vowel teams! When two vowels work together, they usually make one long vowel sound. Let's review all the teams we've learned.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Review all vowel teams. Practice identifying them in words. Reinforce 'two vowels walking' concept while noting exceptions.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Vowel teams have exceptions and ambiguities. Focus on most common patterns. Build familiarity through reading practice.

worksheet_content.practice:
OI makes /oi/ sound (like 'oil')
oil, coin, boil, join, point

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Now we're learning diphthongs! OI makes the /oi/ sound - your mouth moves as you say it: /oi/. Like in 'oil' and 'coin'.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Diphthong = two vowel sounds gliding together. OI usually in middle/end of syllable. Consistent sound.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Diphthongs are generally easier than variable vowel teams. OI is very consistent - always /oi/ sound.

worksheet_content.practice:
OY makes /oi/ sound (same as OI)
boy, toy, joy, Roy, enjoy

teaching_notes.what_to_say: OY is another spelling for the /oi/ sound! Same sound as OI, but OY usually comes at the END of words. Both say /oi/: coin, boy.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Spelling pattern: OI in middle (coin, boil), OY at end (boy, joy). Both = /oi/ sound. Similar to AI/AY pattern.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Confusing OI and OY placement. Teach general rule: OI middle, OY end. Same sound, different positions.

worksheet_content.practice:
OU makes /ow/ sound (like 'ouch!')
out, loud, house, mouse, shout

teaching_notes.what_to_say: OU makes the /ow/ sound like when you say 'ouch!' or 'out'. Your mouth opens wide: /ow/.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Diphthong /ow/ as in 'out, house, loud'. Note: OU can make other sounds too (soup, country), but /ow/ is most common.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: OU has multiple sounds. Start with /ow/ (out, house). Teach exceptions later (soup, through, country, cough).

worksheet_content.practice:
OW can also make /ow/ sound (like 'cow')
cow, now, down, town, brown

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Remember OW can make TWO sounds! We learned long O (snow). Now here's the /ow/ sound like in 'cow' and 'down'. Same sound as OU!
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: OW diphthong /ow/ (cow, now) vs. OW long O (snow, show). Context helps determine which. Often /ow/ in middle, long O at end (not absolute).
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: OW ambiguity very challenging. Students must try both sounds. Frequent exposure to common words helps build pattern recognition.

worksheet_content.practice:
AR makes /ar/ sound (R controls the A)
car, star, park, barn, card

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Now we're learning about Bossy R! When R comes after a vowel, it changes the vowel sound. AR makes /ar/ like in 'car' and 'star'. The R is bossy - it controls the A!
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: R-controlled vowels: R changes preceding vowel sound. AR = /ar/ (not short A or long A). Very consistent pattern.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: May try to say short A then /r/ separately. Teach AR as one sound unit: /ar/. Use 'Bossy R' terminology.

worksheet_content.practice:
OR makes /or/ sound (R controls the O)
for, corn, fork, storm, short

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Another Bossy R! OR makes /or/ like in 'for' and 'corn'. The R controls the O sound.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: R-controlled OR = /or/. Very consistent. Teach as single sound unit.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Fairly straightforward. OR consistently makes /or/. Reinforce Bossy R concept.

title: R-Controlled: ER, IR, UR
phonics_concept: ER, IR, UR all make /er/ sound

worksheet_content.practice:
ER, IR, UR all make the same /er/ sound!
her, bird, turn, fern, girl, burn

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Here's something special! ER, IR, and UR all make the SAME sound: /er/! They all sound alike but are spelled differently. Like 'her', 'bird', and 'turn' - all say /er/!
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: All three spell /er/ sound. ER most common, IR and UR less so. Spelling must be memorized - sound doesn't distinguish them.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Since all three sound identical, spelling is challenging. Students must memorize which spelling for each word. Reading is easier than spelling.

title: Review: All R-Controlled Vowels
phonics_concept: AR, OR, ER, IR, UR review

worksheet_content.tracing: Write R-controlled: ar or er ir ur
worksheet_content.practice:
Match pattern to word:
ar-car, or-for, er-her, ir-bird, ur-turn
worksheet_content.writing: Write one word for each R-controlled pattern

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Great job with Bossy R! When R comes after a vowel, it changes how the vowel sounds. Let's review all the R-controlled vowel patterns.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Review AR (/ar/), OR (/or/), and ER/IR/UR (all /er/). Reinforce that R 'bosses' the vowel. Practice identifying patterns.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: R-controlled vowels change familiar vowel sounds, which can be confusing initially. Continued practice builds mastery.

worksheet_content.practice:
IGH makes long I sound (GH is silent)
high, night, right, light, fight

teaching_notes.what_to_say: IGH is a special pattern! Three letters that make the long I sound. The GH is completely silent. Like in 'night' and 'light'.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Advanced pattern. I makes long I sound, GH is silent. Common in English. Teach as unit to memorize.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Trying to pronounce the GH. Emphasize: GH is SILENT in -IGH pattern. Only the I sound (long I) is heard.

worksheet_content.practice:
TION makes /shun/ sound
ac-tion, na-tion, sta-tion, frac-tion

teaching_notes.what_to_say: -TION is a common ending! Four letters that make /shun/ sound like in 'action' and 'nation'. It's a suffix that makes words into nouns.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Advanced pattern. -TION = /shun/. Very common suffix. Helps decode multisyllabic words. Break into syllables: ac-tion.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: May try to sound out each letter. Teach -TION as chunk making /shun/. Practice syllable division.

worksheet_content.practice:
SION usually makes /shun/ or /zhun/
mis-sion (/shun/), vi-sion (/zhun/)

teaching_notes.what_to_say: -SION is like -TION! It usually makes /shun/ like 'mission', but sometimes /zhun/ like 'vision'. Both are common endings.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: -SION after consonant usually /shun/ (mission, mansion). After vowel often /zhun/ (vision, division). Advanced pattern.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Distinguishing /shun/ vs /zhun/ can be subtle. Context and word familiarity help. Focus on recognition in reading.

title: Advanced: Soft C
phonics_concept: C makes /s/ before E, I, Y

video_links.title: Hard and Soft C - Jack Hartmann

worksheet_content.tracing: Trace soft C words: cent city ice
worksheet_content.practice:
C makes /s/ before E, I, Y:
cent, city, ice, race, cycle

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Remember C can make two sounds! We learned /k/ like 'cat'. Now C makes /s/ before E, I, or Y. Like 'cent', 'city', 'cycle'. This is called 'soft C'.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Soft C rule: C = /s/ before e, i, y. Hard C = /k/ before a, o, u. Important spelling/reading pattern.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Forgetting soft C rule and using /k/ sound. Teach rule explicitly: C before e/i/y says /s/.

title: Advanced: Soft G
phonics_concept: G makes /j/ before E, I, Y

video_links.title: Hard and Soft G - Jack Hartmann

worksheet_content.tracing: Trace soft G words: gem giant gym
worksheet_content.practice:
G makes /j/ before E, I, Y:
gem, giraffe, giant, cage, gym

teaching_notes.what_to_say: G can also make two sounds! We learned /g/ like 'goat'. Now G makes /j/ before E, I, or Y. Like 'gem', 'giraffe', 'gym'. This is 'soft G'.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Soft G rule: G = /j/ before e, i, y (usually). Hard G = /g/ before a, o, u. Less consistent than soft C (exceptions: get, give, girl).
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Soft G has more exceptions than soft C. Teach as general pattern with exceptions to memorize.

worksheet_content.practice:
Consonant+LE makes final syllable:
ta-ble, ap-ple, pur-ple, lit-tle

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Words ending in consonant + LE divide before the consonant. The LE makes the /ul/ sound. Like 'table' = ta-ble, 'apple' = ap-ple.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: C+LE final stable syllable. Divide before consonant: ta/ble, pur/ple. -LE sounds like /ul/. Helps decode multisyllabic words.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Syllable division can be confusing. Practice dividing: consonant goes with -LE to make final syllable.

title: Review: Multisyllabic Words
phonics_concept: Putting it all together - longer words

worksheet_content.tracing: Divide into syllables: rain-bow some-thing but-ter-fly
worksheet_content.practice:
Break down longer words:
rain-bow (2), some-thing (2), but-ter-fly (3), el-e-phant (3)

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Now you know SO many phonics patterns! When you see a long word, break it into smaller chunks or syllables. Sound out each part, then blend them together!
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Teach syllable division strategies. Use all learned patterns to decode longer words. Chunk into manageable parts.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Longer words can feel overwhelming. Break down systematically. Identify known patterns within the word.

worksheet_content.tracing: Trace compound words: sunshine rainbow bedroom
worksheet_content.practice:
Make compound words:
sun + shine = ?, rain + bow = ?, bed + room = ?

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Compound words are two small words put together to make one bigger word! Like 'sun' + 'shine' = 'sunshine'. If you can read each small word, you can read the compound word!
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Teach to identify two separate words within compound. Sound out each part, then put together. Makes longer words easier.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Students may not recognize the two separate words. Explicitly show the division and meaning of each part.

worksheet_content.practice:
Match contractions to full words:
can't = can not, don't = do not, I'm = I am

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Contractions are shortcuts! Two words squeezed together with some letters left out. The apostrophe shows where letters are missing. 'Can not' becomes 'can't'.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Teach common contractions. Show original two words. Explain apostrophe replaces missing letters. Important for reading fluency.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: May not understand apostrophe's function. Explicitly show which letters are removed and where apostrophe goes.

worksheet_content.practice:
Add prefixes:
do → undo, happy → unhappy, make → remake
worksheet_content.writing: Write 3 words with UN or RE prefix

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Prefixes are word parts added to the BEGINNING that change the meaning. UN- means 'not' or 'opposite' (unhappy = not happy). RE- means 'again' (redo = do again).
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Teach prefix meanings. Show base word + prefix. Helps decode and understand longer words. Builds vocabulary.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: May not understand how prefix changes meaning. Explicitly teach meaning of each prefix and how it modifies base word.

video_links.title: Suffixes ED and ING - Jack Hartmann

worksheet_content.practice:
Add suffixes:
jump → jumped, jumping
play → played, playing

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Suffixes are word parts added to the END. -ED shows past tense (jumped = already happened). -ING shows happening now (jumping = right now).
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Teach suffix meanings and sounds. -ED can sound like /ed/, /d/, or /t/. -ING always /ing/. Important for reading verbs.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: -ED has three sounds (jumped /t/, played /d/, wanted /ed/). Teach students to try all three to find real word.

video_links.title: Plural S and ES - Jack Hartmann

worksheet_content.practice:
Make plurals:
cat → cats, dog → dogs, box → boxes, wish → wishes

teaching_notes.what_to_say: To make more than one (plural), we usually add -S (cats, dogs). But after S, X, Z, CH, SH, we add -ES (boxes, wishes, buzzes).
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Teach plural rules. -S for most words, -ES after sibilants (s, x, z, ch, sh). Important reading and spelling pattern.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Knowing when to use -S vs -ES. Teach the rule: -ES after hissing/buzzing sounds.

worksheet_content.tracing: Trace KN words: knee knife know
worksheet_content.practice:
Silent K before N:
knee, knife, know, knock (sound like: nee, nife, no, nock)

teaching_notes.what_to_say: English has some sneaky silent letters! When K comes before N at the start of a word, the K is SILENT. 'Knee' sounds like 'nee', 'knife' like 'nife'.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Teach KN- pattern: K silent. Limited number of KN words - can be memorized. Historically K was pronounced.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Trying to pronounce the K. Emphasize K is completely silent in KN- words. Just say /n/ sound.

worksheet_content.tracing: Trace WR words: write wrong wrap
worksheet_content.practice:
Silent W before R:
write, wrong, wrap, wrist (sound like: rite, rong, rap, rist)

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Another silent letter pattern! When W comes before R at the start of a word, the W is SILENT. 'Write' sounds like 'rite', 'wrong' like 'rong'.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Teach WR- pattern: W silent. Limited WR words. Useful to know for reading and spelling.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Trying to say /w/ before /r/. Emphasize W is silent - just say /r/ sound.

worksheet_content.practice:
GH is silent, I makes long I sound:
night, light, fight, right, bright

teaching_notes.what_to_say: We learned this before! In -IGHT words, the GH is completely silent. Only the long I sound is heard. Common pattern in English.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Review -IGHT pattern from earlier lesson. Reinforce GH silence. Very common, important to master.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Attempting to pronounce GH. Reiterate: GH is always silent in -IGHT pattern.

title: Homophones
phonics_concept: Words that sound the same but have different spellings

worksheet_content.practice:
Match to meanings:
to = toward, two = 2, too = also
hear = with ears, here = this place
worksheet_content.writing: Use each word in a sentence

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Homophones are tricky! They SOUND exactly the same but are SPELLED differently and MEAN different things. You need to know the meaning to spell them correctly.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Teach common homophone sets. Emphasize meaning determines spelling. Important for reading comprehension and writing.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Homophones are confusing for all learners. Requires memorization and understanding of meanings. Use in context.

title: Common Irregular Words 1
phonics_concept: High-frequency words that don't follow rules

worksheet_content.tracing: Trace irregular words: said does were
worksheet_content.practice:
Memorize these tricky words:
said (not 'sayd'), does (not 'doze'), were (not 'weer')

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Some words in English don't follow the phonics rules - they're irregular or 'tricky words'. We have to memorize them. Like 'said' - it doesn't sound like it looks!
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Teach high-frequency irregular words. These must be memorized as sight words. Essential for reading fluency.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Students will try to sound out irregularly. Explain these are exceptions - must be learned by sight/memory.

worksheet_content.tracing: Trace: have come some who what
worksheet_content.practice:
Learn by heart:
have, come, some, who, what
worksheet_content.writing: Write each word 3 times and use in a sentence

teaching_notes.what_to_say: More irregular words to learn! These are used ALL the time in reading, so knowing them by heart helps you read faster and smoother.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Continue building sight word vocabulary. Practice in context. Use flashcards, games for memorization.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Irregular words require repetition and practice. Use multisensory methods to aid memorization.

title: Reading Fluency 1
practice_words: Practice reading sentences smoothly without pausing at each word

worksheet_content.tracing: No tracing - focus on reading practice
worksheet_content.practice:
Read these sentences smoothly:
The cat sat on the mat.
I like to play with my dog.
We went to the park.
worksheet_content.writing: Write your own sentence and read it fluently

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Now that you know phonics, let's practice reading SMOOTHLY! Good readers don't stop after every word - they read in phrases with expression, like they're talking.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Model fluent reading. Have child practice repeated reading of same text. Focus on phrasing, expression, and speed.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Choppy, word-by-word reading. Encourage reading in phrases. Use punctuation as guides for pausing.

title: Reading Comprehension 1
practice_words: Read and answer questions about simple stories

video_links.title: Story Questions
video_links.description: Answering who, what, where, when, why

worksheet_content.practice:
Read the story, then answer:
Who? What? Where? When? Why?
worksheet_content.writing: Retell the story in your own words

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Reading isn't just saying the words - it's UNDERSTANDING the story! After you read, think about what happened. Who was in the story? What did they do?
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Teach basic comprehension strategies: visualizing, predicting, questioning, summarizing. Ask questions during and after reading.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Reading without comprehension. Stop frequently to check understanding. Encourage retelling and discussion.

worksheet_content.practice:
Read a story book and an information book.
How are they different?
worksheet_content.writing: Which type do you like better and why?

teaching_notes.what_to_say: There are different kinds of books! Fiction books are made-up stories. Nonfiction books teach us real information about the world. Both are fun to read!
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Expose to both fiction and nonfiction. Teach text features (headings, captions, diagrams in nonfiction). Build diverse reading skills.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Focusing only on fiction. Balance both types to build well-rounded readers who can handle various texts.

title: Review: Decoding Strategies
phonics_concept: Tools for figuring out unknown words
practice_words: Sound it out, look for chunks, context clues, ask for help

worksheet_content.practice:
When you see a hard word, try:
1. Sound it out
2. Look for chunks you know
3. Read the sentence for clues
4. Ask for help
worksheet_content.writing: Find 3 hard words in a book and use strategies

teaching_notes.what_to_say: You have so many tools now! When you see a word you don't know: try sounding it out, look for word parts you recognize, use the sentence to guess, or ask someone. Good readers use ALL these strategies!
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Teach flexible strategy use. Model trying different approaches. Encourage independence while providing support.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Over-reliance on one strategy (e.g., only sounding out). Teach multiple approaches and when to use each.

title: Building Reading Stamina
phonics_concept: Reading longer books and sustained practice
practice_words: Practice reading for increasingly longer periods

worksheet_content.practice:
Reading time tracker:
Start with 5 minutes, build to 15-20 minutes of independent reading
worksheet_content.writing: Reading log: What did you read today?

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Real readers read for a long time! Let's build up your reading muscles. Start with just a few minutes, then slowly read for longer and longer periods.
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Set reading time goals. Start short, gradually increase. Make it enjoyable - let child choose books they love.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Expecting too much too soon. Build stamina gradually. Celebrate progress. Keep it positive and engaging.

worksheet_content.practice:
Try reading:
- Adventure stories
- Animal books
- Science books
- Funny stories
Which do you like?
worksheet_content.writing: Make a list of books you want to read

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Reading is FUN when you read about things you love! Try lots of different kinds of books to find your favorites. Adventure? Animals? Space? There's a book for everyone!
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Expose to variety of genres and topics. Help child develop preferences. Visit library. Make reading a joyful, lifelong habit.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Forcing books child doesn't enjoy. Let them choose (within appropriate level). Reading should be pleasurable!

book_recommendations.title: Books good for reading aloud
book_recommendations.level: At or below reading level for fluency

worksheet_content.practice:
Read a book to someone:
- Parent
- Sibling
- Pet
- Stuffed animal
Tell them about the story!
worksheet_content.writing: Write about your favorite book to recommend to a friend

teaching_notes.what_to_say: Reading is even more fun when you share it! Read your favorite books to your family, friends, even your pet! Then talk about what you read. What did you like? What was your favorite part?
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Encourage reading aloud to build fluency and confidence. Facilitate book discussions. Make reading a social, shared activity.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Reading as solitary activity only. Foster community around reading - share, discuss, recommend books.

title: Celebration: You Are a Reader!
phonics_concept: Reflecting on reading journey and next steps

worksheet_content.practice:
Think about:
- Your first reading lesson
- Hard words you can now read
- Favorite books you've read
- What you want to read next
worksheet_content.writing: Write: I am a reader! I can read ___________!

teaching_notes.what_to_say: CONGRATULATIONS! You've completed your phonics journey! You started by learning letter sounds, and now you can read real books! You are officially a READER! Keep reading every day and you'll get even better. The world of books is open to you now!
teaching_notes.how_to_guide: Celebrate this major milestone! Review the journey from letter sounds to reading books. Set goals for continued reading growth. Emphasize this is the BEGINNING, not the end - reading is a lifelong skill and joy.
teaching_notes.common_mistakes: Thinking phonics instruction is 'done'. Continue supporting reading development, introduce more complex texts, build vocabulary, deepen comprehension. Reading instruction continues beyond phonics foundation.
